g.data-narration: Welcome to Liang-Barsky Line Clipping Algorithm - an efficient parametric method for clipping lines in computer graphics.
Line clipping removes portions of lines outside a rectangular window, essential for rendering only visible graphics elements efficiently.
g.text: Removing portions of line segments that lie outside a
rectangular clipping window. Used in rendering pipelines,
GUIs, CAD systems for real-time performance.

g.data-narration: The Liang-Barsky algorithm uses parametric equations to compute exact clipping intersections efficiently without repeated subdivisions.
g.text: • Liang-Barsky: Parametric approach - calculates exact intersection points
• Cohen-Sutherland: Outcode approach - may require multiple subdivisions
• Efficiency: O(1) per line vs potential multiple iterations

g.data-narration: A line from P1 to P2 is represented using parameter t where t=0 is P1 and t=1 is P2, allowing us to find any point along the line.
g.text: A line from P1(x₁, y₁) to P2(x₂, y₂):

g.data-narration: The rectangular clipping window has four boundaries: left xwmin, right xwmax, bottom ywmin, and top ywmax. Points must satisfy all four conditions.
g.text: For a point (x,y) to be INSIDE, all conditions must hold:

g.data-narration: Substituting parametric equations into boundary conditions gives four inequalities that determine if points on the line are inside the window.
g.text: Substituting x = x₁ + tΔx and y = y₁ + tΔy (where Δx = x₂-x₁, Δy = y₂-y₁):

g.data-narration: P-values indicate line direction relative to each boundary (negative=entering, positive=exiting). Q-values measure starting point distance from each boundary.
g.text: Delta Values: P and Q Values

g.data-narration: The sign of p values determines if a line enters (p<0) or exits (p>0) each boundary. Q values tell us if the starting point is inside or outside.
g.text: When p < 0: Line ENTERS the window through this boundary
When p > 0: Line EXITS the window through this boundary
When p = 0: Line is parallel to this boundary

g.data-narration: The algorithm initializes t0=0 and t1=1, then iterates through each boundary updating these parametric values based on p and q signs.
g.text: 5. If valid, calculate endpoints using t₀ and t₁

g.data-narration: For each boundary: if p equals zero and q is negative, reject the line. Otherwise, update t0 or t1 based on whether p is negative or positive.
After processing all four boundaries, if t0 is greater than t1, the line lies completely outside the window and should be rejected.
g.text: IF t₀ > t₁ → Line is COMPLETELY OUTSIDE
IF t₀ ≤ t₁ → Line is VISIBLE

g.data-narration: Example 1: A line from (2,2) to (5,5) with window (0,0) to (10,10). Since all p and q values allow full range, the line remains unchanged.
Example 2: Line from (5,15) to (15,5) clips to a single point at (10,10) where it touches the top-right corner of the window.
Example 3: Line from (-5,-5) to (-2,-2) is completely outside the window. Since t0 exceeds t1 after processing, the line is rejected.
The algorithm flow shows initialization, boundary checks with p and q values, updating t parameters, and validity checking to determine clipping results.
Remember these essential formulas: parametric equations, p and q values, t update rules, and the rejection condition for exam success.
